title: The Mongol Empire
description: This exercise will focus on contextualization and CCOT of developments in the Mongol Empire, assuming students have limited understanding of the ECN theme. It will have 3 types based on the themes GOV, ECN, CDI, scaled 4x (total 12).
topic_path: The Mongol Empire and the Making of the Modern World | 2.2

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following political developments has the most enduring impact on the growth of networks of exchange in Afro-Eurasia between 1200 and 1450 CE? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: growth of large empires
competition between regional states
growth of city-states
centralized bureaucracy

assessment_items.question_content:
**What development could a historian use to support the idea that trade routes grew to meet the demand for specialized goods? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Silk production techniques were known only to highly skilled artisans in China.
Common goods like ammonium chloride, spices, and precious metals were widely available throughout Eurasia by 1200 CE.
Minting techniques for paper money were specialized in China and spread as far as the city-state of Venice, Italy by 1200 CE.
New shipbuilding techniques created larger and faster ships.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.
**

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following provides the best historical context for the purpose of the stone structure pictured above? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A large stone courtyard with an arched entrance to an inner building.
options.caption: Source: This is a picture of the interior of Sultan Han, a thirteenth-century Seljuk caravanserai (or *han*) in modern Turkey, similar to those built along the Silk Roads. CC BY-SA 2.0.

choices.content: Trade routes were dangerous, and merchants needed safe places to rest.
Trade routes led to increased wealth in cities along each route.
Trade routes fostered the cultural exchange of Muslim architecture.
Trade routes were safe, and merchants traveled many weeks to meet at caravanserai.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the image below to answer the question that follows.
**

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is one shared development across all long-distance networks of exchange between 1200 and 1450 CE?
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A large stone courtyard with an arched entrance to an inner building.
options.caption: Source: This is a picture of the interior of Sultan Han, a thirteenth-century Seljuk caravanserai (or *han*) in modern Turkey, similar to those built along the Silk Roads. CC BY-SA 2.0,

choices.content: Cities were centers of production and trade.
States controlled most industrial production.
Traders set prices to determine the market value of goods.
Local markets were spaces of cultural conflict.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

> The emperor’s mint is in this city of Khanbaliq; and it is set up in such a way that you might well say he has mastered the art of alchemy, as I will now prove to you ... he has money made for him in the following way. He has the bark stripped from trees—to be precise, from the mulberry trees whose leaves are eaten by silkworms. Then the thin layer [that is] between the bark and the wood of the tree is removed. After being ground and pounded, it is pressed with the aid of glue into sheets like those of cotton paper, which are completely black. And when these sheets are ready, they are cut up into pieces of different sizes, rectangular in shape and of greater length than breadth. ... All these sheets of paper are stamped with the emperor’s seal.

From *Travels* by Marco Polo (1254–1324), written around 1300 CE, recounting his travel to the capital city of Khanbaliq in Yuan China. 

**Which of the following represents the broad historical context for the development described in the excerpt above? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]



[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Citation: Polo, Marco. *The Travels.* Translated by Nigel Cliff. London: Penguin Classics, 2019.

choices.content: Technological innovations allowed for financial innovation.
Financial innovations allowed for new methods of taxation.
Technological innovations allowed long-distance travel.
Financial innovations allowed states to mint their own currency.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Use the excerpt below to answer the question that follows.**

> The emperor’s mint is in this city of Khanbaliq; and it is set up in such a way that you might well say he has mastered the art of alchemy, as I will now prove to you ... he has money made for him in the following way. He has the bark stripped from trees—to be precise, from the mulberry trees whose leaves are eaten by silkworms. Then the thin layer [that is] between the bark and the wood of the tree is removed. After being ground and pounded, it is pressed with the aid of glue into sheets like those of cotton paper, which are completely black. And when these sheets are ready, they are cut up into pieces of different sizes, rectangular in shape and of greater length than breadth. ... All these sheets of paper are stamped with the emperor’s seal.

From *Travels* by Marco Polo (1254–1324), written around 1300 CE, recounting his travel to the capital city of Khanbaliq in Yuan China. 

**What is the best example of how developments like the one described in the excerpt directly impacted trade along the Silk Roads between 1200 and 1450 CE? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]



[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Citation: Polo, Marco. *The Travels.* Translated by Nigel Cliff. London: Penguin Classics, 2019.

choices.content: Traders could carry more goods.
Merchants resisted using new types of currency.
City economies grew with larger markets.
Travelers exchanged ideas across long distances.

assessment_items.question_content:
**
Which of the following provides the context for how the Mongol Empire rose to power between 1200 and 1450 CE? 
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: changes in the climate, mastery of horse warfare, and charismatic leadership
the collapse of neighboring empires, farming technology, and religious pluralism
networks of exchange, technological innovations, and religious unity
centralized bureaucracy, an all-powerful emperor, and financial innovation

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Mongol Empire is the largest contiguous land empire in history, stretching across much of Eurasia. What is the best description of their system of governance over this enormous territory? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: decentralized with much authority resting in appointed local governors and officials
centralized around the Great Khan, who ruled from the Khanate in China
decentralized with power going to ruling families in city-states across the empire
centralized around a leader with much authority resting on elected local officials

assessment_items.question_content:
**The Mongols encouraged trade across Eurasia by repressing bandits and encouraging the growth of trading centers. Which of the following groups was impacted most directly by these developments?
**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: merchants
priests
elites
artisans

assessment_items.question_content:
**What development could a historian use to support the idea that under the Mongol Empire trade routes fostered cultural and intellectual exchange?  **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Italian city-states adopted the Arabic number system.
The Korean script adopted Chinese pictographs.
Multiple religions were practiced in Samarkand.
Scholars in Baghdad were encouraged to continue intellectual work.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Mongol society and culture were remarkably open, accommodating diversity as a way to build strength. Which of the following is the best example of an effect of this process?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Foreign scholars and physicians attained positions of power in the Mongol court.
The Arabic number system was adopted throughout many European cities.
Chinese bureaucrats were replaced because they resisted Mongol leadership.
Merchants attained a higher social class under the Mongols.

assessment_items.question_content:
**During the century-long period of peace under the Mongols, there was an unprecedented connection across Afro-Eurasia. What was an unintended consequence of this development between 1200 and 1450 CE? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Bubonic plague was able to spread quickly and efficiently.
Smallpox decimated nearly half of the population in some areas.
More people in Afro-Eurasia had resistance to disease.
Mongol invasions wiped out millions of people.
